planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; southern wax myrtle is one of the most versatile and useful native shrubs of the Gulf South — salt-tolerant, drought-tolerant once established, adaptable to wet or dry soils, fast-growing, nitrogen-fixing, and productive of waxy berries used for bayberry candles and incense.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; minimal care once established. Morella cerifera (southern wax myrtle or bayberry) is one of the most ecologically important and garden-useful native shrubs of the Gulf Coastal Plain, native from New Jersey south to Florida and west to Texas and Oklahoma. The aromatic, semi-evergreen to evergreen leaves (crushed, they release a distinctive bayberry-bay-spice fragrance) are used dried in wreaths, sachets, and as a flavoring herb similar to bay laurel in coastal Southern cooking. The small, silvery-waxy, blue-gray berries produced on female plants are the basis of bayberry candle wax — melted from the berries and cast into candles with the distinctive bayberry fragrance beloved in New England and Colonial American tradition. Wax myrtle is among the most adaptable native plants for Gulf Coast gardens: it tolerates salt spray, wet conditions, drought, poor soils, and coastal conditions equally well, making it outstanding for beach landscapes, rain gardens, wet corners, and dry banks alike. The plant fixes nitrogen through root symbiosis with Frankia bacteria (actinorrhizal nitrogen fixation), improving soil fertility. As a screening shrub, hedge, or small tree, it grows rapidly (2–3 feet per year when young) to 10–15 feet. Native birds eat the waxy berries and nest in the dense foliage.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Shrubs
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Native Landscape Plants

planting.note: Plant container stock in fall (the preferred planting time for California native plants) in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, gravelly or sandy soil in full sun; purple sage is a California coastal sage scrub native adapted to summer drought — water only until established (first winter), then rely on rainfall in zone 8–10; excessive irrigation is the primary cause of death for established plants.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, dry, gravelly or sandy soil in full sun; water sparingly — established plants need summer dry conditions to thrive. Salvia leucophylla (purple sage or coastal sage) is a California native aromatic shrub from the coastal sage scrub and chaparral communities of coastal and inland California. The plant produces aromatic, wrinkled, silver-gray to white-felted leaves with a strong, distinctive fragrance — more complex and resinous than common garden sage (S. officinalis), with additional notes of camphor, eucalyptus, and warm spice. The soft, gray foliage creates a beautiful silvery landscape effect, especially dramatic when paired with purple lavender and other Mediterranean-climate plants. In late spring through early summer, it produces whorled clusters of pink to light-purple flowers on white-felted stems — both fragrant and attractive to hummingbirds and bumblebees. Purple sage is a classic plant of the California chaparral and is notable for producing volatile allelopathic compounds (camphor, 1,8-cineole) from its leaves that inhibit the germination and growth of competing plants beneath its canopy — a natural herbicide effect. The leaves are traditionally used by California indigenous peoples for ceremonial smudging, and can be used as a culinary sage substitute (though stronger). Drought tolerance once established is extreme.
sources: Las Pilitas Nursery — California Native Plants

planting.note: Plant root divisions or container stock in spring in well-drained, deeply tilled, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, deep, sandy-loam soil in full sun; licorice root requires a deep, well-drained, fertile growing site and 2–3 years of growth before the roots are ready to harvest — the sweet, anise-flavored roots contain glycyrrhizin, a compound 50 times sweeter than sucrose, used in confectionery, pharmaceuticals, and herbal medicine.

directions: Plant root divisions in deeply tilled, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, deep soil in full sun; harvest roots in fall of years 2–3. Glycyrrhiza glabra (licorice root or Spanish licorice) is the plant behind all licorice flavoring in candy, herbal teas, and medicine — a perennial Mediterranean and Central Asian leguminous herb producing sweet, tan-skinned taproot stubs extending 3–4 feet into the soil. The roots contain glycyrrhizin, a saponin compound approximately 30–50 times sweeter than sucrose, responsible for the characteristic sweet, anise-like, distinctive licorice flavor. Licorice root has been used medicinally for over 4,000 years — in ancient Egypt, China, India, and Greece — as a treatment for coughs, digestive disorders, and ulcers; it remains one of the most widely used herbal medicines worldwide, used in pharmaceuticals as a sweetening and flavoring agent for bitter medicines (the name 'licorice' comes from the Greek for 'sweet root'). CAUTION: Overconsumption of licorice root or glycyrrhizin can cause hypertension, potassium loss, and hormonal effects — not for excessive use, and problematic for those with high blood pressure or heart conditions. As a garden plant, licorice grows as a multi-stemmed, pinnately-leafed, attractive perennial shrub with pale lavender flowers — harvest roots in the fall of year 2 or 3 for best glycyrrhizin content.
sources: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants — Licorice
University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Herbs

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–November in zone 8–9) in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy soil in full sun; oats are one of the most widely used cool-season cover crops in the South and Mid-Atlantic — fast-establishing, winter-hardy (though killed by hard freezes below 10–20°F, leaving behind weed-suppressing mulch), and excellent for building organic matter before spring planting.

directions: Broadcast or drill at 2–3 lbs per 1,000 sq ft in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in fall; mow or incorporate in spring before planting summer crops. Avena sativa (common oat or cereal oat) is one of the world's most important cereal grain crops and one of the most widely used cool-season cover crops across North America. As a grain crop, oats are distinguished by their extraordinarily high soluble fiber (beta-glucan) content, which has been clinically demonstrated to reduce LDL cholesterol — making oat consumption one of the most evidence-based dietary interventions for cardiovascular health. Steel-cut oats, rolled oats, and oat flour are kitchen staples worldwide. As a cover crop in zone 6–10, oats are outstanding for fall planting: they establish quickly in cool weather, grow vigorously to 2–4 feet, suppress weeds effectively through the winter, and are either winter-killed by hard freezes (leaving a weed-suppressing mulch that requires no tillage to terminate — a self-terminating cover crop) or mowed down in spring before summer planting. Oats also scavenge residual soil nitrogen, preventing leaching over winter. In zone 8–9, oats typically winter-kill at temperatures below 15–20°F, providing a built-in termination; in milder areas, mow or incorporate before heading. Mix with hairy vetch or field peas for a nitrogen-fixing, carbon-building combination cover crop.
sources: USDA SARE — Cover Crop Encyclopedia — Oats
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cover Crops
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Oat Breeding

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring (NOT from seed — true French tarragon is a sterile clone and cannot be grown from seed; seed-grown plants sold as 'tarragon' are usually the nearly flavorless Russian tarragon) in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun; French tarragon requires excellent drainage and air circulation, struggling in the humid Gulf South — best treated as a cool-season herb in zone 9.

directions: Plant verified divisions (never seed — true French tarragon produces no viable seed) in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun; divide clumps every 2–3 years to maintain vigor. Artemisia dracunculus var. sativa (French tarragon or true tarragon) is the culinary tarragon of classic French cooking — one of the four fines herbes (with chervil, parsley, and chives) fundamental to French cuisine, and the primary flavoring of tarragon vinegar, Béarnaise sauce, French mustard, and tarragon chicken. Its defining characteristic is the uniquely complex, sweet-anise, slightly bitter, herbal flavor derived from estragole and ocimene — an utterly distinctive taste impossible to replicate with any substitute. CRITICAL PURCHASE NOTE: True French tarragon cannot be grown from seed — it is a sterile triploid clone that produces no viable seed. Virtually all 'tarragon seeds' sold commercially produce Russian tarragon (A. dracunculus), an almost flavorless species with little culinary value. Always purchase verified French tarragon as a rooted plant or division from a reputable culinary herb nursery, never from seed packets. In the garden, French tarragon performs best in well-drained, lean, dry conditions with good air circulation — the humid, wet Gulf Coast summer is challenging; treat it as a cool-season herb in zone 9, growing in containers with excellent drainage and bringing it out of summer heat.
sources: Herb Society of America — French Tarragon
University of California Cooperative Extension — Culinary Herbs
